Mercedes C-Class C200 CDI / C200d Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes C‑Class C200 CDI / C200d engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned engine typically costs £2,500–£5,500 supply only depending on generation, with the OM646 lower and the OM654 higher. An OM651 replacement costs on average £2,797; an OM654 costs on average £3,592. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,600. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your C‑Class.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes C200 CDI / C200d engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends which generation your car is. The OM646 (W203, 2000–2007) is widely regarded as one of Mercedes' most robust engines — capable of 500,000+ miles. The OM651 (W204/W205, 2007–2018) has timing chain stretch and injector issues. The OM654 (W205 Facelift/W206, 2018+) is more modern but early versions have EGR cooler and valvetrain watchpoints. Check the Era Map above for your specific car.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the C200 CDI / C200d engine?

acceptedAnswer.text: OM646: injector seal failure ("Black Death") and turbo wear. OM651: timing chain stretch (rear‑mounted, costly to replace) and piezo injector failures. OM654: EGR cooler failures, valvetrain wear (camshafts/tappets), turbocharger failures, and oil leaks.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a C200 CDI / C200d engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and generation. A W203 C200 CDI is a £1,000–£4,000 vehicle — a £2,500–£3,500 recon is 63–350% of the car's value; replacement often exceeds our 70% Mercedes scrap threshold. A W205/W206 C200d is a £10,000–£25,000 vehicle — a £4,000–£5,000 recon is 16–50% of the car's value; replacement is viable. Evaluate carefully based on your car's generation and condition.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes C200 CDI / C200d engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The C200 CDI covers OM646 (W203, 2000–2007), OM651 (W204/W205, 2007–2018), and OM654 (W205 Facelift/W206, 2018+). Tell us your registration date and we'll confirm which one you have for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I prevent the common failures on a C200 CDI / C200d?

acceptedAnswer.text: On OM646: replace injector seals at the first sign of chuffing. On OM651: timing chain replacement at 80,000–100,000 miles is recommended; regular oil changes with high‑quality oil every 5,000–10,000 miles are essential. On OM654: regular oil changes with MB‑spec oil and shortened intervals are essential. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, chuffing or coolant loss, book an inspection immediately.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes C‑Class C200 CDI / C200d Engine Replacement Costs
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the range across all generations; specific costs vary by era — see the Era Map above. An OM651 replacement engine costs on average £2,797 in good condition, with new or reconditioned units up to £3,295. An OM654 replacement engine costs on average £3,592.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a C200 CDI / C200d engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer). OM651 timing chain replacement is complex and costly as the chain is at the back of the engine.

cta.label: → Compare C200 CDI / C200d engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Injector Seal Failure — "Black Death" (OM646, W203 2000–2007)
problems.symptoms: Ticking or chuffing noise from the injector area, black carbon buildup around injectors, rough idle, loss of power, diesel smell in the cabin.
problems.repairCost: £200–£600 (injector seal replacement, plus cleaning)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if you hear chuffing or smell diesel

problems.recommendation: Injector seal failure — known as "Black Death" — is the OM646's most common issue. The seals degrade and allow combustion gases to escape, creating black carbon deposits around the injectors. If you hear a chuffing noise or smell diesel in the cabin, have the injector seals replaced immediately. Catching it early prevents damage to the injector and cylinder head. With proper maintenance, these engines can exceed 500,000 miles.

problems.title: Timing Chain Stretch (OM651, W204 2007–2014 & W205 2014–2018)
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start (2–5 seconds after starting), metallic ticking, engine warning light, loss of power, chain rattle on acceleration.
problems.repairCost: £1,200–£2,800 (chain, guides and tensioner replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The OM651's timing chain is a known weak point. The chain stretches quickly, producing a distinct rattle during engine operation. The chain is at the back of the engine, making replacement technically complex and costly. Regular oil changes with high‑quality oil and intervals of 5,000–10,000 miles are essential. At the first sign of cold‑start rattle, have the chain inspected immediately — ignoring it can lead to catastrophic engine failure.

problems.title: EGR Cooler & Valvetrain Wear (OM654, W205 Facelift 2018–2021 & W206 2021–present)
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss, white exhaust smoke, rough idling, burning smell, engine warning light, limp mode.
problems.repairCost: £600–£1,500 (EGR cooler replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: N/A — repair only (if caught before overheating)

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant levels drop or symptoms appear

problems.recommendation: Early OM654 engines have reported EGR cooler failures, valvetrain wear (camshafts and tappets), turbocharger failures, and oil leaks at the high‑pressure fuel pump seal. Regular oil changes with the correct MB‑spec oil and shortened intervals are essential. At the first sign of coolant loss, white smoke or rough running, have the EGR and valvetrain inspected.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: C200 CDI / C200d — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Catch it early — before it damages the injector or head
Catch it early — before chain snaps
Catch it early — before it causes overheating
Replace or rebuild — cheaper than collateral damage
Little left to salvage — replacement is the only option

buyingChecks.title: Buying This C200 CDI / C200d? Check These First
buyIf: * OM646 (W203): clean injectors (no "Black Death") — full service history
* OM651 (W204/W205): timing chain replaced or inspected — regular 5,000–10,000‑mile oil changes
* OM654 (W205 Facelift/W206): post‑2019 with improved valvetrain — good service history
* Full Mercedes service history with correct MB‑spec oil
avoidIf: * OM646: chuffing noise or diesel smell — "Black Death" injector seal failure — walk away
* OM651: cold‑start rattle with no chain history — timing chain may be failing — walk away
* OM654: coolant loss with no diagnosis — EGR cooler may be failing
* Poor service history — all these engines are sensitive to neglect

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The C200 CDI / C200d badge is a journey through Mercedes diesel evolution — from the legendary OM646 to the modern OM654. The OM646 is the most robust, capable of 500,000+ miles with injector seals as the main watchpoint. The OM651 is more refined but has timing chain stretch and injector issues — regular oil changes are non‑negotiable. The OM654 is the most modern but early versions have EGR, valvetrain and turbo watchpoints. Buy on full service history — and never ignore a cold‑start rattle.

cta.label: → Compare C200 CDI / C200d engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes C‑Class C200 CDI / C200d (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling; timing chain indicator (OM651)
* Injector Area — Check for black carbon buildup around injectors; "Black Death" indicator (OM646)
* Coolant Level — Check for unexplained loss; EGR cooler indicator (OM654)
* Service History — including which generation (W203/W204/W205/W206) and confirmed oil‑change intervals
* Smoke Check — White smoke can indicate injector or EGR issues
* Turbo Health — Listen for whistling or whining; turbo failure indicator
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles?
* MOT History — Check for emissions‑related failures
